title: Interpolation 2 images
content_html:
Interpolation 2 images

Interpolation between 2 images

Here’s a compact starter + a full HTML explainer you can download.

TL;DR (quickest path) • Fastest crossfade in Processing (GPU): draw A, then draw B with tint(255, alpha) where alpha = 255 * t. • Best-looking fade: do it gamma-correct (linear-light) via a tiny fragment shader. • Export to video: save PNG frames, then ffmpeg -r 30 -i out/frame%04d.png -c:v libx264 -crf 18 out.mp4.

Minimal Processing (fast GPU crossfade)

*// CrossfadeTwoImages.pde*


PImage imgA, imgB;
int frames = 150;          

*// number of transition frames*


int w = 3840, h = 2160;    

*// adjust for your target resolution*



void settings() { size(w, h, P2D); }
void setup() {
  surface.setTitle("Two-Image Crossfade");
  imgA = loadImage("A.jpg"); 

*// or PNG; alpha is respected*


  imgB = loadImage("B.jpg");
  imageMode(CORNER);
  frameRate(30);
}

void draw() {
  float t = constrain((float)frameCount / frames, 0, 1);

  

*// Draw A*


  pushStyle();
  tint(255, 255);
  image(imgA, 0, 0, width, height);

  

*// Draw B with alpha*


  int alpha = int(255 * t);
  tint(255, alpha);
  image(imgB, 0, 0, width, height);
  popStyle();

  saveFrame("out/frame####.png");
  if (frameCount >= frames) exit();
}

Gamma-correct version (nicer tonality)

Use a fragment shader to interpolate in linear light.

GammaCorrectCrossfade.pde

PImage imgA, imgB;
PShader crossfade;
int frames = 150;
int w = 3840, h = 2160;

void settings() { size(w, h, P2D); }
void setup() {
  surface.setTitle("Gamma-Correct Crossfade");
  imgA = loadImage("A.jpg");
  imgB = loadImage("B.jpg");
  crossfade = loadShader("crossfade.frag");
  frameRate(30);
}

void draw() {
  float t = constrain((float)frameCount / frames, 0, 1);
  crossfade.set("t", t);
  crossfade.set("texA", imgA);
  crossfade.set("texB", imgB);
  shader(crossfade);
  image(imgA, 0, 0, width, height);  

*// any quad; shader samples both*


  resetShader();
  saveFrame("out/frame####.png");
  if (frameCount >= frames) exit();
}

data/crossfade.frag


#ifdef GL_ES
precision mediump float;
precision mediump int;
#endif

uniform sampler2D texA;
uniform sampler2D texB;
uniform float t;
varying vec4 vertTexCoord;



*// approximate sRGB <-> linear*


vec3 srgb_to_linear(vec3 c)  { return pow(c, vec3(2.2)); }
vec3 linear_to_srgb(vec3 c)  { return pow(c, vec3(1.0/2.2)); }

void main() {
  vec2 uv = vertTexCoord.st;
  vec4 a = texture2D(texA, uv);
  vec4 b = texture2D(texB, uv);

  

*// premultiply alpha so PNG transparency blends cleanly*


  vec3 a_rgb = srgb_to_linear(a.rgb) * a.a;
  vec3 b_rgb = srgb_to_linear(b.rgb) * b.a;
  float a_a = a.a;
  float b_a = b.a;

  vec3 mix_rgb = mix(a_rgb, b_rgb, t);
  float mix_a  = mix(a_a,  b_a,  t);

  vec3 out_rgb = mix_a > 0.0 ? mix_rgb / mix_a : vec3(0.0);
  out_rgb = linear_to_srgb(out_rgb);
  gl_FragColor = vec4(out_rgb, mix_a);
}

Export frames to video (FFmpeg)

*# from the sketch folder*


ffmpeg -r 30 -i out/frame%04d.png -s 3840x2160 \
  -pix_fmt yuv420p -crf 18 -preset medium -c:v libx264 crossfade_uhd_30fps.mp4

Extras you might want • Alignment first (homography / landmarks) to reduce ghosting, then crossfade. • Feature-based morph (Delaunay triangles warped per-frame, then blend). • Optical-flow morph (dense flow A→B, warp + blend) for that “melting” look. • UHD performance: stick to P2D and shaders; avoid CPU per-pixel loops at 4K.

⸻

Download the full HTML explainer (Apple Notes/GitHub-ready)

It includes everything above plus FFmpeg one-liners, CPU reference code, and notes on alpha, gamma, and UHD.

Download the HTML explainer

If you’d like, I can also package this as a tiny Processing project folder with data/ placeholders ready to drop your two images into.

The code needs to be optimised for static image generation. The aim is to use it to create interpolations between a large series of images, which will then be stitched together to effectively create a slow motion video

Perfect—let’s optimise this for static frame generation at scale (so you can stitch everything later into a slow-motion video) and keep it GPU-fast, low-memory, and resumable.

What you’re getting • A Processing batch interpolator that: • Loads only the current pair of images (low RAM). • Generates INTER_FRAMES in-betweens per pair (plus optional HOLD_START/HOLD_END). • Uses a gamma-correct GLSL shader (better tonality than naïve sRGB blends). • Writes sequentially numbered PNGs for easy stitching and resume. • Logs a compact manifest.csv (which frames came from which pair). • A concise HTML README with usage and FFmpeg commands.

Download • Processing sketch folder: /mnt/data/BatchImageInterpolator • Main file: BatchImageInterpolator.pde — batch logic + config • Shader: data/crossfade.frag — gamma-correct crossfade (premultiplied-alpha aware) • Output folders you’ll create: input/ (your ordered images), out/ (frames) • README (HTML) — setup, tuning, FFmpeg examples

One-time setup (super short) 1. Open the sketch in Processing. 2. Create input/ inside the sketch folder and drop your ordered images there (e.g., img_0001.jpg, img_0002.jpg, …). 3. In BatchImageInterpolator.pde, edit the Quick Config block: • INTER_FRAMES → how many in-betweens per pair (e.g., 30). • HOLD_START / HOLD_END → duplicate still frames around each transition. • CANVAS_W/H → output resolution (e.g., UHD 3840×2160). • FIT_MODE → FIT (letterbox), FILL (crop), or STRETCH. • EASING → linear, smoothstep, easeInOutCubic. • OVERWRITE=false to resume safely. 4. Run. Frames appear in out/ as frame_000000.png, frame_000001.png, …

Why this is optimised for static output • Streaming pairs: only two PImages in RAM at any time. • GPU path: no CPU per-pixel loops; a single quad with a shader. • Gamma-correct: blends in linear light; avoids muddy mid-tones. • Resumable: scans out/ to pick the next frame index; set OVERWRITE=false. • Deterministic: strict filename ordering → easy FFmpeg stitching.

Stitch to slow-mo video later (examples)

*# 25 fps H.264 (good delivery quality)*


ffmpeg -r 25 -i out/frame%06d.png -c:v libx264 -crf 18 -preset medium -pix_fmt yuv420p slow_interpolation_25fps.mp4



*# 25 fps ProRes 422 HQ (archival / NLE-friendly)*


ffmpeg -r 25 -i out/frame%06d.png -c:v prores_ks -profile:v 3 slow_interpolation_25fps_prores.mov

Tips for very large runs • Set SHOW_PREVIEW=false to reduce draw overhead. • Keep OVERWRITE=false so you can stop/restart anytime without losing work. • Use HOLD_* to slow the feel without exploding the number of pairs. • Keep inputs consistently sized and ordered; the sorter is by filename.

⸻

Need me to add: • Homography alignment (auto-register per pair) before blending, • Triangle-mesh morph (feature points → Delaunay → affine per triangle), or • Optical-flow morph (flow-based warp + blend)

…so your in-betweens feel more “morph-like” than “crossfade-like”? I can drop in optional modules next.

Download BatchImageInterpolator

Got it — here’s your full Processing sketch, shader, and README bundled together.

[⬇️](sandbox:/mnt/data/BatchImageInterpolator.zip?_chatgptios_conversationID=68b58a7b-a69c-8321-8a64-240da57a969c&_chatgptios_messageID=d9a0ee9f-1c57-4005-84e2-8509c3d07839) **[ Download BatchImageInterpolator (ZIP)](sandbox:/mnt/data/BatchImageInterpolator.zip?_chatgptios_conversationID=68b58a7b-a69c-8321-8a64-240da57a969c&_chatgptios_messageID=d9a0ee9f-1c57-4005-84e2-8509c3d07839)**

The ZIP contains: • BatchImageInterpolator.pde — main Processing code (batch static frame interpolator). • data/crossfade.frag — GLSL shader for gamma-correct blending. • Batch_Image_Interpolator_README.html — usage guide with config, FFmpeg tips, and workflow notes. • (create input/ for your source images, out/ will be generated automatically).

Would you like me to also include a ready-made “input/” folder with two placeholder images so the sketch runs immediately for testing?

Explain: 

Homography alignment (auto-register per pair) before blending,
	•	Triangle-mesh morph (feature points → Delaunay → affine per triangle), or
	•	Optical-flow morph (flow-based warp + blend)

Great question — these three techniques are ways of going beyond simple crossfades, so your interpolations look like natural morphs instead of just double-exposed blends. I’ll break each down clearly, with an HTML-ready explanation you can reuse in your notes or project docs.

⸻

Image Alignment & Morphing Methods

1. Homography alignment (auto-register per pair before blending)

What it is: A homography is a 3×3 matrix that maps one plane in an image onto another. If your two images are of the same flat scene (e.g., a poster, painting, or building façade) but taken from slightly different viewpoints, a homography can align them.

Workflow: 1. Detect keypoints in both images (e.g., SIFT, ORB). 2. Match keypoints (nearest-neighbor search). 3. Estimate the homography matrix H using RANSAC (robust to outliers). 4. Warp image B using H to align it with image A. 5. Crossfade between A and the warped B.

Effect: Reduces ghosting — instead of “double eyes” or “double letters,” features align and fade smoothly. Best for planar surfaces or when the camera motion is a rotation/translation around a single scene.

⸻

2. Triangle-mesh morph (feature points → Delaunay → affine per triangle)

What it is: A feature-based morph that uses manually (or automatically) chosen control points, then warps both images toward an intermediate shape before blending.

Workflow: 1. Choose corresponding points (e.g., left eye corner in A ↔ left eye corner in B). • This can be manual (GUI) or automatic (face landmark detectors). 2. Compute the average positions of these points for a given interpolation time t. 3. Triangulate the point set (usually with Delaunay triangulation). 4. For each triangle: • Compute the affine transform from A’s triangle → intermediate triangle. • Compute the affine transform from B’s triangle → intermediate triangle. • Warp pixels from A and B into that triangle and blend by factor t. 5. Assemble warped triangles into the full frame.

Effect: Produces “true morphs” where shapes (faces, objects) gradually shift and transform instead of just fading. Classic method in film VFX (Beier–Neely morphs).

⸻

3. Optical-flow morph (flow-based warp + blend)

What it is: Optical flow estimates a dense pixel-wise motion field between two frames: for each pixel in A, a vector tells you where it moves to in B.

Workflow: 1. Compute dense optical flow A→B (e.g., Farnebäck, TV-L1, or deep learning models like RAFT). 2. For a time t: • Warp A forward by t \cdot \text{flow}. • Warp B backward by (1−t) \cdot \text{flow}. 3. Blend the warped images. 4. Repeat for intermediate timesteps.

Effect: Produces the smoothest “liquid morphs” — objects bend, stretch, and flow from one to another. But: optical flow is heavy to compute and can create artefacts (tearing, “melting”) if the flow is wrong or occlusions exist.

⸻

Comparison Table

Method Data Needed Complexity Best For Artefacts / Limitations
Homography Keypoints (auto) Low Flat/planar scenes, small camera motion Only works if scene ≈ planar; 3D objects misalign
Triangle-mesh Feature points (manual/auto) Medium Faces, structured objects Needs good correspondences; triangles may fold
Optical-flow Dense motion (auto) High Arbitrary scenes, smooth morphs Flow estimation errors → warping artefacts

⸻

Visual Metaphor • Homography = “lining up two transparencies” on a light table. • Triangle-mesh = “cutting images into puzzle pieces and reshaping them.” • Optical-flow = “melting one image into the other like wax.”

⸻

👉 Would you like me to **integrate one of these methods into your BatchImageInterpolator sketch** (e.g., auto-homography alignment using OpenCV before blending), or just keep them as conceptual notes for now? 
Download html as conceptual notes

All set. Here are your conceptual notes as a clean HTML file:

[⬇️](sandbox:/mnt/data/alignment_and_morphing_concepts.html?_chatgptios_conversationID=68b58a7b-a69c-8321-8a64-240da57a969c&_chatgptios_messageID=fccc00a6-13ce-440c-baa2-fc7d976c1eed) **[ Download: Alignment & Morphing Concepts (HTML)](sandbox:/mnt/data/alignment_and_morphing_concepts.html?_chatgptios_conversationID=68b58a7b-a69c-8321-8a64-240da57a969c&_chatgptios_messageID=fccc00a6-13ce-440c-baa2-fc7d976c1eed)**

Contents: • Homography alignment (auto-register per pair) — workflow, best use, limits • Triangle-mesh morph (feature points → Delaunay → affine per triangle) • Optical-flow morph (flow-based warp + blend) • Comparison table + practical notes on blending (gamma-correct, premultiplied alpha) and multi-band seams

If you want, I can extend this with lightweight pseudocode blocks for OpenCV/Processing integrations next.

Explain Optical-flow morph in more detail. Implementation needs to be pure Processing. Suggest any additional libraries

Optical-flow morph (Processing-only): how it works + code you can drop in

Below is a practical, Processing-native approach for generating in-between frames using dense optical flow. I’ll show: 1. What optical flow is + the morph equation 2. A production-ish pipeline (multi-scale, masks, hole-fill) 3. A pure Processing reference implementation (Horn–Schunck dense flow + CPU bilinear warping) suitable for offline static frame generation 4. Suggested Java libraries you can add to Processing for higher quality/robustness (still “Processing” because it’s Java on the JVM)

⸻

1) Concept in one minute • Dense optical flow gives you a 2-D motion vector F(x,y) = (u,v) for (almost) every pixel, mapping where pixels in A are expected to move to in B. • For an in-between time t ∈ [0,1], you warp A forward by t·F and B backward by (1−t)·F, then blend:

I_t(x,y) ;=; (1-w);A\big(x - t\cdot u(x,y),,y - t\cdot v(x,y)\big) ;+; w;B\big(x + (1-t)\cdot u(x,y),,y + (1-t)\cdot v(x,y)\big)

where w is typically t (or use gamma-correct, premultiplied blending for better tonality/alpha handling).

⸻

2) Pipeline you can trust for static (offline) rendering 1. Preprocess • Convert to linear-light working space if you care about tonality (or keep sRGB and do gamma-correct blending later). • Mild denoise / Gaussian blur σ≈0.5–1.0 can stabilise flow. 2. Multi-scale (image pyramid) • Build L levels (coarsest → finest). • Estimate flow at the coarsest level (easy motion), then upsample & refine at each finer level. 3. Flow estimation (Horn–Schunck here; Farnebäck/TV-L1 if you add OpenCV) • Iteratively minimise a data term (brightness constancy) + smoothness term (regularisation). • 50–150 iterations per scale is common for offline frames. 4. Forward–backward consistency (optional) • Compute flow A→B and B→A; flag pixels where they disagree → likely occlusions. • Use these flags to down-weight the unreliable side during blending. 5. Warp & blend • Bilinear sampling from A and B at sub-pixel coordinates. • Prefer premultiplied + gamma-correct blending to avoid muddy mids and dark fringes. 6. Hole-filling • Small holes (due to disocclusions) can be filled with edge-aware interpolation (quick fallback: repeated bilateral/median where mask = hole).

⸻

3) Pure Processing reference: Dense Horn–Schunck + CPU warper

This is designed for offline static frame generation (not real-time). It’s compact and readable rather than hyper-optimised; for UHD you’ll want to (a) reduce iteration counts, (b) run multi-scale, and (c) consider tiling or using a library.

Files assume two same-size images A.jpg, B.jpg inside data/. You can integrate this into your BatchImageInterpolator by replacing the crossfade with morphFrame(...).

3.1 Sketch shell




*// OpticalFlowMorphHS.pde*


PImage imgA, imgB;
int W = 1280, H = 720;      

*// set to your working resolution*


int LEVELS = 3;             

*// pyramid levels (coarsest to finest)*


int HS_ITERS = 80;          

*// iterations per level*


float HS_ALPHA = 40.0;      

*// smoothness (bigger = smoother flow)*



void settings() { size(W, H, P2D); }
void setup() {
  imgA = loadImage("A.jpg");
  imgB = loadImage("B.jpg");
  imgA.resize(W, H);
  imgB.resize(W, H);

  

*// Build Gaussian pyramids*


  PImage[] pyrA = buildPyr(imgA, LEVELS);
  PImage[] pyrB = buildPyr(imgB, LEVELS);

  

*// Compute dense flow u,v from A->B (start at coarsest)*


  float[][] u = zeros(W, H);
  float[][] v = zeros(W, H);

  for (int lvl = LEVELS-1; lvl >= 0; lvl--) {
    int w = pyrA[lvl].width, h = pyrA[lvl].height;

    

*// Upscale flow to current level (skip for coarsest)*


    if (lvl < LEVELS-1) {
      u = upscale(u, w, h);
      v = upscale(v, w, h);
    } else {
      u = zeros(w, h);
      v = zeros(w, h);
    }

    

*// Convert images to grayscale float arrays*


    float[][] a = toGrayArray(pyrA[lvl]);
    float[][] b = toGrayArray(pyrB[lvl]);

    

*// Horn–Schunck refinement at this scale*


    hornSchunck(a, b, u, v, HS_ALPHA, HS_ITERS);
  }

  

*// Generate a few in-between frames*


  background(0);
  for (int i = 0; i <= 30; i++) {
    float t = i/30.0;
    PImage frame = morphFrame(imgA, imgB, u, v, t, true 

*/*gammaCorrect*/*

, true 

*/*premult*/*

);
    image(frame, 0, 0);
    saveFrame("out/morph_##.png");
  }
  println("done");
  exit();
}

3.2 Utility: pyramids, arrays, filtering


PImage[] buildPyr(PImage img, int levels) {
  PImage[] pyr = new PImage[levels];
  pyr[0] = img.get();
  for (int i=1; i<levels; i++) {
    PImage prev = pyr[i-1];
    PImage g = prev.copy();
    g.filter(BLUR, 1); 

*// mild blur before downsample*


    PImage d = createImage(max(1, prev.width/2), max(1, prev.height/2), ALPHA);
    g.resize(d.width, d.height);
    d.copy(g, 0, 0, g.width, g.height, 0, 0, d.width, d.height);
    pyr[i] = d;
  }
  return pyr;
}

float[][] zeros(int w, int h) {
  float[][] z = new float[h][w];
  return z;
}

float[][] toGrayArray(PImage p) {
  p.loadPixels();
  float[][] g = new float[p.height][p.width];
  for (int y=0; y<p.height; y++) {
    int row = y * p.width;
    for (int x=0; x<p.width; x++) {
      int c = p.pixels[row + x];
      

*// sRGB luma approximation*


      float r = red(c)/255.0, gg = green(c)/255.0, b = blue(c)/255.0;
      g[y][x] = 0.2126*r + 0.7152*gg + 0.0722*b;
    }
  }
  return g;
}

float[][] upscale(float[][] a, int newW, int newH) {
  float[][] out = new float[newH][newW];
  int h = a.length, w = a[0].length;
  for (int y=0; y<newH; y++) {
    float fy = (y * (h-1.0)) / max(1, newH-1.0);
    int y0 = floor(fy), y1 = min(y0+1, h-1); float wy = fy - y0;
    for (int x=0; x<newW; x++) {
      float fx = (x * (w-1.0)) / max(1, newW-1.0);
      int x0 = floor(fx), x1 = min(x0+1, w-1); float wx = fx - x0;
      float v00 = a[y0][x0], v01 = a[y0][x1], v10 = a[y1][x0], v11 = a[y1][x1];
      out[y][x] = mix(mix(v00, v01, wx), mix(v10, v11, wx), wy);
    }
  }
  return out;
}

float mix(float a, float b, float t) { return a + (b - a) * t; }

3.3 Horn–Schunck dense flow (single-scale iteration)


void hornSchunck(float[][] A, float[][] B, float[][] u, float[][] v, float alpha, int iters) {
  int h = A.length, w = A[0].length;

  

*// Gradients (Ix, Iy) via simple Sobel; It = B - A*


  float[][] Ix = sobelX(A);
  float[][] Iy = sobelY(A);
  float[][] It = new float[h][w];
  for (int y=0; y<h; y++) for (int x=0; x<w; x++) It[y][x] = B[y][x] - A[y][x];

  

*// Iterative refinement*


  float a2 = alpha*alpha;
  float[][] uAvg = new float[h][w];
  float[][] vAvg = new float[h][w];

  for (int k=0; k<iters; k++) {
    boxAverage(u, uAvg);  

*// local smoothness (4/8-neighbourhood)*


    boxAverage(v, vAvg);

    for (int y=1; y<h-1; y++) {
      for (int x=1; x<w-1; x++) {
        float ix = Ix[y][x], iy = Iy[y][x], it = It[y][x];
        float denom = a2 + ix*ix + iy*iy;
        

*// brightness constancy term*


        float P = ix*uAvg[y][x] + iy*vAvg[y][x] + it;
        

*// update*


        u[y][x] = uAvg[y][x] - (ix * P) / denom;
        v[y][x] = vAvg[y][x] - (iy * P) / denom;
      }
    }
  }
}



*// Simple 3x3 box average*


void boxAverage(float[][] src, float[][] dst) {
  int h = src.length, w = src[0].length;
  for (int y=1; y<h-1; y++) {
    for (int x=1; x<w-1; x++) {
      float s = 0;
      for (int j=-1; j<=1; j++)
        for (int i=-1; i<=1; i++)
          s += src[y+j][x+i];
      dst[y][x] = s / 9.0;
    }
  }
  

*// copy edges*


  for (int x=0; x<w; x++) { dst[0][x] = src[0][x]; dst[h-1][x] = src[h-1][x]; }
  for (int y=0; y<h; y++) { dst[y][0] = src[y][0]; dst[y][w-1] = src[y][w-1]; }
}

float[][] sobelX(float[][] a) {
  int h = a.length, w = a[0].length;
  float[][] g = new float[h][w];
  int[] kx = {-1,0,1,-2,0,2,-1,0,1};
  for (int y=1; y<h-1; y++) for (int x=1; x<w-1; x++) {
    float s=0; int t=0;
    for (int j=-1; j<=1; j++) for (int i=-1; i<=1; i++,t++) s += a[y+j][x+i]*kx[t];
    g[y][x]=s/8.0;
  }
  return g;
}

float[][] sobelY(float[][] a) {
  int h = a.length, w = a[0].length;
  float[][] g = new float[h][w];
  int[] ky = {-1,-2,-1,0,0,0,1,2,1};
  for (int y=1; y<h-1; y++) for (int x=1; x<w-1; x++) {
    float s=0; int t=0;
    for (int j=-1; j<=1; j++) for (int i=-1; i<=1; i++,t++) s += a[y+j][x+i]*ky[t];
    g[y][x]=s/8.0;
  }
  return g;
}

3.4 Warping + gamma-correct, premultiplied blending


PImage morphFrame(PImage A, PImage B, float[][] u, float[][] v, float t,
                  boolean gammaCorrect, boolean premult) {
  int w = A.width, h = A.height;
  PImage out = createImage(w, h, ARGB);
  A.loadPixels(); B.loadPixels(); out.loadPixels();

  for (int y=0; y<h; y++) {
    for (int x=0; x<w; x++) {
      float uf = u[y][x], vf = v[y][x];

      

*// sample coords*


      float ax = x - t*uf, ay = y - t*vf;
      float bx = x + (1.0 - t)*uf, by = y + (1.0 - t)*vf;

      int ca = bilinear(A, ax, ay);
      int cb = bilinear(B, bx, by);

      

*// unpack*


      float Aa = alpha(ca)/255.0, Ar = red(ca)/255.0, Ag = green(ca)/255.0, Ab = blue(ca)/255.0;
      float Ba = alpha(cb)/255.0, Br = red(cb)/255.0, Bg = green(cb)/255.0, Bb = blue(cb)/255.0;

      if (gammaCorrect) {
        Ar = pow(Ar, 2.2); Ag = pow(Ag, 2.2); Ab = pow(Ab, 2.2);
        Br = pow(Br, 2.2); Bg = pow(Bg, 2.2); Bb = pow(Bb, 2.2);
      }

      if (premult) {
        Ar *= Aa; Ag *= Aa; Ab *= Aa;
        Br *= Ba; Bg *= Ba; Bb *= Ba;
      }

      float wB = t;            

*// can use confidence mask here; default: t*


      float wA = 1.0 - wB;

      float R = wA*Ar + wB*Br;
      float G = wA*Ag + wB*Bg;
      float Bl= wA*Ab + wB*Bb;
      float Aout = wA*Aa + wB*Ba;

      if (premult && Aout > 1e-6) {
        R /= Aout; G /= Aout; Bl /= Aout;
      }
      if (gammaCorrect) {
        R = pow(max(0, R), 1.0/2.2);
        G = pow(max(0, G), 1.0/2.2);
        Bl= pow(max(0, Bl), 1.0/2.2);
      }

      out.pixels[y*w + x] = color(constrain(R*255,0,255), constrain(G*255,0,255), constrain(Bl*255,0,255), constrain(Aout*255,0,255));
    }
  }
  out.updatePixels();
  return out;
}

int bilinear(PImage img, float fx, float fy) {
  int w = img.width, h = img.height;
  if (fx < 0) fx = 0; if (fy < 0) fy = 0;
  if (fx > w-1) fx = w-1; if (fy > h-1) fy = h-1;

  int x0 = floor(fx), y0 = floor(fy);
  int x1 = min(x0+1, w-1), y1 = min(y0+1, h-1);
  float tx = fx - x0, ty = fy - y0;

  int c00 = img.pixels[y0*w + x0];
  int c01 = img.pixels[y0*w + x1];
  int c10 = img.pixels[y1*w + x0];
  int c11 = img.pixels[y1*w + x1];

  float a00=alpha(c00), r00=red(c00), g00=green(c00), b00=blue(c00);
  float a01=alpha(c01), r01=red(c01), g01=green(c01), b01=blue(c01);
  float a10=alpha(c10), r10=red(c10), g10=green(c10), b10=blue(c10);
  float a11=alpha(c11), r11=red(c11), g11=green(c11), b11=blue(c11);

  float a0 = mix(a00, a01, tx), a1 = mix(a10, a11, tx);
  float r0 = mix(r00, r01, tx), r1 = mix(r10, r11, tx);
  float g0 = mix(g00, g01, tx), g1 = mix(g10, g11, tx);
  float b0 = mix(b00, b01, tx), b1 = mix(b10, b11, tx);

  return color(mix(r0, r1, ty), mix(g0, g1, ty), mix(b0, b1, ty), mix(a0, a1, ty));
}

Performance note: For UHD, consider: • Reducing HS_ITERS and increasing LEVELS (e.g., 4–5 levels, 30–50 iters/level). • Doing the warping in a shader (upload flow as a 2-channel float texture encoded in RG). • Running in headless (no preview) and saving frames to disk (your BatchImageInterpolator pattern).

⸻

4) “Still Processing” but stronger: add a Java CV library

If you want higher quality, robustness, and speed without leaving Processing (still Java): • BoofCV (pure Java, easy to add as a Processing library): • Pros: No native binaries, good documentation, has optical-flow algorithms (e.g., Horn–Schunck variants, KLT tracking, dense/discrete flow). • How: Download BoofCV jars, put in code/ folder of your sketch, import classes and call the dense flow. • Use case: dependable pure-Java baseline with multi-scale flow and decent speed. • OpenCV for Processing (wrapper) or JavaCV (OpenCV bindings): • Pros: Access to Farnebäck, TV-L1, DIS optical flow, pyramidal LK, feature detection, homography, etc. • Cons: Native binaries (platform-specific), slightly more setup. • How: Install the Processing OpenCV library (or include JavaCV/Bytedeco jars + natives in code/), then call calcOpticalFlowFarneback(...) / DualTVL1OpticalFlow_create() equivalents.

Recommended path: • Start with the pure Processing Horn–Schunck above to prove the pipeline + warping/blending. • If you like the results but want cleaner motion on tougher content, switch the hornSchunck(...) step to BoofCV dense flow (small code changes) or OpenCV Farnebäck/TV-L1 for more robustness.

⸻

Want me to also make a GPU warper variant (uploading the flow to a shader) for faster UHD runs?

Here you go — a GPU warper variant that keeps the heavy per-frame work on the GPU while caching per-pair flow on disk: • ⬇️ Download: BatchImageInterpolator_FlowGPU.zip Contents: • BatchImageInterpolator_FlowGPU.pde — batch driver, computes/loads flow (CPU), builds RG flow texture, renders frames via shader • data/morph_flow.frag — GLSL shader (decodes flow, warps A/B, gamma-correct premult blend) • README_FlowGPU.html — quick setup + FFmpeg command

How it works (quick recap) • For each pair, we compute Horn–Schunck flow (CPU, once), cache it as out/flows/flow_XXXXXX.bin. • We pack (u,v) into an 8-bit RG texture with a per-pair flowScale (max |u| or |v| in pixels). • The shader decodes flow to UV offsets, samples A at uv − t*d and B at uv + (1−t)*d, and blends in linear light with premultiplied alpha. • Result: UHD-friendly, super fast per-frame generation.

If you want a version that uses float textures (GL-only, more precision) or swaps HS for BoofCV/OpenCV flow while keeping the same GPU warp, say the word and I’ll package that too.

I’ve packaged a static, no-draw-loop batch generator that does everything inside setup() and exits when done. • ⬇️ StaticBatchGenerator.zip Contents: • StaticBatchGenerator.pde — runs the entire batch in setup() (no draw()), then exit() • data/crossfade.frag — gamma-correct crossfade shader (for MODE="CROSSFADE") • data/morph_flow.frag — GPU flow warper shader (for MODE="FLOW_GPU") • README_Static.html — quick usage notes

How it behaves • Processes all adjacent image pairs from input/, normalises them to your canvas (FIT/FILL/STRETCH), and writes sequential PNGs to out/. • Modes: • CROSSFADE — gamma-correct fades via shader. • FLOW_CPU — pure Processing Horn–Schunck multi-scale flow + CPU warp/blend. • FLOW_GPU — compute flow once (CPU), pack to RG texture, warp on GPU per frame. • Per-pair flow caching in out/flows/flow_XXXXXX.bin, so re-runs skip recomputation. • Resume-safe: it scans out/ to continue numbering unless OVERWRITE=true.

Set up quickly 1. Put ordered source images in input/. 2. Open StaticBatchGenerator.pde; set: • MODE = "FLOW_CPU" (or "FLOW_GPU" / "CROSSFADE") • CANVAS_W/H, INTER_FRAMES, HOLD_START/END, FIT_MODE. 3. Run. Files land in out/. Stitch with:

ffmpeg -r 25 -i out/frame%06d.png -c:v libx264 -crf 18 -preset medium -pix_fmt yuv420p static_batch_25fps.mp4
